questions.retrievalTag: Trichotillomania Non-Specific Hair Pulling r/o Fungus
questions.reviewPearl: Hair in different lengths strongly suggests trichotillomania versus a medical disease.
questions.stem: A 14-year-old girl has patchy hair loss. Exam reveals scalp hair of varying lengths without erythema or scaling. KOH prep is negative. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Identify trichotillomania by the presence of hair of varying lengths after ruling out fungal causes.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer: C) Trichotillomania
body: Trichotillomania involves pulling out hair to reduce anxiety, classically leaving hair of different lengths. Fungal infections must be ruled out first.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Alopecia and tinea capitis are medical causes of hair loss that do not present with anxiety-induced pulling and varying hair lengths. Body dysmorphia involves perceived flaws, not primary hair pulling.

questions.retrievalTag: Muscle Dysmorphia Preoccupation with muscle size
questions.reviewPearl: Muscle dysmorphia patients are at high risk for rhabdomyolysis and roid rage from excessive exercise and steroids.
questions.stem: A 22-year-old male spends 4 hours daily exercising and uses anabolic steroids, believing his musculature is insufficient despite being heavily muscled. What is the diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Recognize muscle dysmorphia presentation and its associated behaviors like anabolic steroid use.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer: B) Muscle dysmorphia
body: Muscle dysmorphia is a subset of body dysmorphia predominantly in males, characterized by a preoccupation with insufficient muscle size leading to excessive exercise and steroid use.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: While related, typical body dysmorphia focuses on facial/body asymmetry. OCD and anorexia lack the specific male-predominant preoccupation with gaining muscle mass.

questions.retrievalTag: CBT used is Exposure and Response Prevention
questions.reviewPearl: Therapy for OCD either controls the anxiety (desensitization) or redirects the compulsion to something innocuous.
questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman spends 3 hours a day washing her hands due to fears of contamination. She is aware the behavior is irrational. What is the best initial psychotherapy?
questions.educationalObjective: Identify Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (Exposure and Response Prevention) as the best psychotherapy for OCD.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer: B) Cognitive behavioral therapy
body: CBT, specifically Exposure and Response Prevention (ERP), is the most effective therapy for OCD, directed at desensitizing the anxiety associated with the obsession.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Psychodynamic, dialectical, and interpersonal therapies are not first-line treatments for the targeted control of OCD-related anxiety and compulsive rituals.

questions.retrievalTag: Hoarding Distress about thoughts of ridding possessions
questions.reviewPearl: Hoarding disorder can severely compromise patient safety due to fall risks and blocked emergency access. eftab720 tightenfactor0 eftab720 tightenfactor0 Document 2: Impulse Control and Destructive Disorders
questions.stem: An elderly man’s home is cluttered with accumulated trash, causing an inability for emergency personnel to maneuver safely. He feels intense distress at the thought of discarding items. Diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Diagnose hoarding disorder based on the inability to discard possessions resulting in unsafe living environments.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer: A) Hoarding disorder
body: Hoarding disorder involves immense distress about losing items, leading to unsafe, cluttered homes filled with possessions regardless of their actual value.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: OCD compulsions neutralize anxiety but lack the specific distress of discarding useless items. Schizophrenia and aging do not primarily feature this specific retention pathology.

questions.retrievalTag: Kleptomania Steals to reduce anxiety Women SSRIs
questions.reviewPearl: Kleptomaniacs steal unplanned items of no value to reduce anxiety and feel severe remorse, unlike typical theft.
questions.stem: A 25-year-old woman impulsively steals cheap trinkets she can easily afford. She steals spontaneously without planning, feels intense guilt afterward, and stashes the items away. What is the best treatment?
questions.educationalObjective: Identify kleptomania features and select SSRIs combined with group therapy as the management strategy.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer: B) SSRIs and group therapy
body: Kleptomania involves impulsive theft of unneeded items to reduce anxiety, followed by guilt; it is typically managed with SSRIs and psychotherapy.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Incarceration is inappropriate because this is a psychiatric impulse disorder, not premeditated theft. Haloperidol and ECT are not indicated for impulse control disorders.

questions.retrievalTag: Intermittent Explosive Disorder Violent or destructive act out of proportion
questions.reviewPearl: Symptoms of intermittent explosive disorder often lessen with age and can be treated with SSRIs and group therapy.
questions.stem: A 35-year-old man routinely destroys furniture when his child drops a toy. After these disproportionately violent outbursts, he feels calm and relaxed. Diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Diagnose intermittent explosive disorder based on disproportionate destructive reactions to minor stressors.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer: A) Intermittent explosive disorder
body: Intermittent explosive disorder involves violent or destructive acts out of proportion to a minor stressor, followed immediately by feeling calm and without remorse.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Antisocial and borderline personality disorders have pervasive relationship and behavioral issues, not just acute stressor-induced destructive outbursts followed by calm.

questions.retrievalTag: Pyromania Sets fire for pleasure / anxiety
questions.reviewPearl: Pyromania requires ruling out arson; pyromaniacs act to relieve anxiety or seek pleasure, not for financial gain.
questions.stem: A 19-year-old male is arrested for repeatedly setting fires to abandoned sheds. He admits he sets them because it brings him intense pleasure and sexual arousal. Diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Differentiate pyromania (fire setting for pleasure/anxiety reduction) from arson (fire setting for profit).

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer: B) Pyromania
body: Pyromania involves setting fires for pleasure, fascination, or sexual arousal, distinguishing it from arson which is done for monetary gain or revenge.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Arson is committed for monetary gain (insurance). Conduct disorder and schizophrenia do not specifically account for the intense pleasure/arousal derived from fire-setting.

questions.retrievalTag: linked impulse control disorders with depleted serotonin
questions.reviewPearl: Medications and therapy often work poorly for impulse control disorders, but SSRIs remain the standard first trial. eftab720 tightenfactor0 eftab720 tightenfactor0 Document 3: Post-Traumatic Stress Disorders
questions.stem: A patient with an impulse control disorder starts treatment. Which medication class was historically linked to depleted serotonin in these disorders and is commonly trialed?
questions.educationalObjective: Recall the theoretical role of SSRIs in treating impulse control disorders based on serotonin depletion.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer: C) SSRIs
body: DSM-IV linked impulse control disorders with depleted serotonin, making SSRIs the standard pharmacological approach, though efficacy often varies.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Benzodiazepines, antipsychotics, and mood stabilizers are not the primary medications targeted for the depleted serotonin theory in these specific impulse disorders.

questions.retrievalTag: > 3 days AND < 1 month duration = Acute stress
questions.reviewPearl: Early group therapy implementation for Acute Stress Disorder can limit the duration and prevent progression to PTSD.
questions.stem: A woman survived a severe car crash 2 weeks ago. She now experiences nightmares, avoidance of cars, and hypervigilance. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Distinguish Acute Stress Disorder from PTSD based on a symptom duration of less than 1 month.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer: A) Acute stress disorder
body: Acute stress disorder presents with PTSD-like symptoms (intrusion, avoidance, arousal) lasting greater than 3 days but less than 1 month.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: PTSD requires symptoms lasting >1 month. Adjustment disorder follows a non-life-threatening stressor. GAD lacks a specific life-threatening trauma trigger.

questions.retrievalTag: PTSD SSRI/SNRI (adjunct)
questions.reviewPearl: Benzodiazepines are generally avoided in PTSD due to high abuse potential; PTSD is predominated by long-term anxiety.
questions.stem: A combat veteran reports 3 months of flashbacks, depersonalization, irritability, and avoiding loud noises. What is the preferred pharmacological adjunct to group therapy?
questions.educationalObjective: Select SSRI/SNRIs as the preferred pharmacotherapy adjunct for Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer: B) SSRI/SNRIs
body: For PTSD (>1 month duration), group therapy is definitive, and SSRI/SNRIs are the preferred adjunct medications for long-term anxiety management.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Benzodiazepines are easily abused and only indicated for acute panic attacks. Antipsychotics and Lithium are not first-line agents for PTSD.

questions.retrievalTag: Adjustment Disorder Begin < 3 months from stressor
questions.reviewPearl: Adjustment disorder usually requires no pharmacological treatment, as reassurance is often sufficient.
questions.stem: A 40-year-old man develops depressive symptoms 2 months after losing his job. Symptoms are not severe enough for MDD. What is the diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Diagnose adjustment disorder following a non-life-threatening stressor leading to mood changes within 3 months.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer: B) Adjustment disorder
body: Adjustment disorder features mood changes beginning within 3 months of a non-life-threatening stressor (like job loss) and lasts less than 6 months.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: MDD requires full SIGECAPS criteria. Persistent depressive disorder requires 2 years of symptoms. Acute stress disorder requires a life-threatening stressor.

questions.retrievalTag: Too little attachment (RAD)
questions.reviewPearl: Treatment for Reactive Attachment Disorder targets the caregiver through parent coaching and teaching, rather than treating the child directly. eftab720 tightenfactor0 eftab720 tightenfactor0 Document 4: Major Depression and Bipolar Disorder
questions.stem: A 3-year-old adopted from an abusive home fails to bond with her new parents and rarely seeks comfort. Autism is ruled out. Diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Differentiate Reactive Attachment Disorder from DSED based on the complete lack of attachment to caregivers.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer: B) Reactive attachment disorder
body: Reactive attachment disorder (RAD) occurs when severe neglect or abuse in infancy leads to an inability to attach to any adult caregiver.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: DSED involves inappropriate, excessive attachment to strangers. Adjustment disorder and PTSD do not accurately describe this specific infant attachment failure.

questions.retrievalTag: If there are suicidal ideations AND a plan, hospitalize.
questions.reviewPearl: Assessing the intention, ability, and validity of the suicide threat are all paramount before deciding on outpatient therapy.
questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman exhibits anhedonia, fatigue, and insomnia for 3 weeks. She reports a specific plan to end her life. What is the most appropriate next step?
questions.educationalObjective: Manage a suicidal patient with a specific plan by prioritizing immediate hospitalization.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer: C) Hospitalize immediately
body: Patients meeting Major Depressive Episode criteria who have suicidal ideation and a concrete plan must be hospitalized immediately to ensure safety.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Discharging or using a safety contract is unsafe for a patient with a clear suicide plan. ECT is reserved for refractory or catatonic depression, not initial triage.

questions.retrievalTag: Bipolar I is mania-predominant DIG FASTER
questions.reviewPearl: If a patient requires hospitalization or has psychosis during an elevated mood state, the diagnosis is automatically Bipolar I.
questions.stem: A 25-year-old man presents with 8 days of decreased need for sleep, grandiosity, pressured speech, and spending sprees. What is the diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Diagnose Bipolar I disorder based on a full manic episode lasting at least one week.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer: A) Bipolar I
body: Bipolar I is defined by a true manic episode lasting at least 1 week, marked by symptoms like grandiosity, decreased sleep, and impulsivity (DIG FASTER).

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Bipolar II requires hypomania (<7 days, less severe impairment) and major depression. Cyclothymia is a chronic low-grade mood cycle.

questions.retrievalTag: ATYPICAL SLEEP Insomnia/ hyperinsomnia WEIGHT Weight gain
questions.reviewPearl: SSRIs/SNRIs remain first-line for depression and are equally efficacious regardless of typical or atypical features.
questions.stem: A patient reports depressed mood for 3 weeks alongside hypersomnia, hyperphagia, weight gain, and profound fatigue. What is the subtype of this depression?
questions.educationalObjective: Recognize the clinical presentation of atypical depression featuring hypersomnia and hyperphagia.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer: B) Atypical
body: Atypical depression features "atypical" symptoms such as increased sleep, increased appetite, and weight gain, opposite to typical melancholic depression.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Melancholic depression features decreased sleep and appetite. Dysthymia is chronic (>2 years). Disruptive mood dysregulation affects children with behavioral outbursts.

questions.retrievalTag: Dysthymia—Persistent Depressive Disorder depressed mood for 2 years
questions.reviewPearl: Dysthymia is treated identically to MDD: rule out hypothyroidism, screen for suicide risk, and prescribe SSRIs. eftab720 tightenfactor0 eftab720 tightenfactor0 Document 5: Grief, Survivors, and Babies
questions.stem: A 45-year-old feels consistently depressed for 2.5 years, never without symptoms for more than 2 months, but maintains daily functioning. Diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Diagnose Persistent Depressive Disorder (dysthymia) based on chronic, mild depression lasting over 2 years.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer: C) Persistent Depressive Disorder (Dysthymia)
body: Dysthymia (Persistent Depressive Disorder) is chronic depression lasting >2 years without meeting severe MDE criteria, allowing the patient to function.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: MDD involves severe, acute symptoms impairing function. Cyclothymia and Bipolar II both require episodes of hypomania.

questions.retrievalTag: Grief CAN be happy with family... NO psychotic features, or... there IS insight
questions.reviewPearl: Do not medically treat the guilt of normal grief; instead, offer therapy sessions to help the patient process it.
questions.stem: A woman’s husband died 3 months ago. She cries holding his clothes but enjoys visiting grandchildren. She occasionally hears his voice, knowing it isn't real. Diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Differentiate normal grief from major depressive disorder based on insight and the ability to experience happiness.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer: B) Normal grief
body: Normal grief lasts <12 months, waxes and wanes, allows for happiness, and can include psychotic-like features (hearing the deceased) as long as there is insight.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: MDD features pervasive hopelessness. PCBD requires symptoms lasting >12 months. Postpartum psychosis is completely unrelated.

questions.retrievalTag: POSTPARTUM PSYCHOSIS Fears the baby, likely to kill it Psychosis +
questions.reviewPearl: Postpartum psychosis requires immediate intervention to separate mother and baby, alongside treatment with mood stabilizers or antipsychotics.
questions.stem: A mother who delivered 3 weeks ago believes her baby is a demon and fears she must kill it. She is actively hallucinating. Diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Identify postpartum psychosis and recognize the extremely high risk of harm to the infant.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer: C) Postpartum psychosis
body: Postpartum psychosis presents within 1 month with delusions, hallucinations, intense fear of the baby, and a high risk of infanticide.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Postpartum blues and depression lack psychotic features and delusions driving infanticidal ideation. Grief is unrelated to childbirth.

questions.retrievalTag: POSTPARTUM DEPRESSION Doesn’t care about baby Treatment Anti-Depressants
questions.reviewPearl: Postpartum depression differs from baby blues by its severity (meeting MDE criteria) and duration lasting past 2 weeks.
questions.stem: A woman is 1 month postpartum and reports daily depressed mood, anhedonia, and not caring about her baby, but denies hallucinations. Treatment?
questions.educationalObjective: Diagnose and treat postpartum depression using antidepressant medications.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer: B) SSRIs
body: Postpartum depression meets MDD criteria, presents within 1 month, and often involves mom not caring about the baby. SSRIs are the indicated treatment.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Baby blues require no treatment but resolve within 2 weeks. Mood stabilizers are for psychosis/bipolar. ECT is an extreme measure not first-line here.

questions.retrievalTag: DEPRESSION Pervasive Global Cannot imagine happy
questions.reviewPearl: In depression following loss, the sadness transitions away from the deceased and becomes pervasive in all aspects of the patient's life. eftab720 tightenfactor0 eftab720 tightenfactor0 Document 6: Eating Disorders
questions.stem: A 65-year-old man’s wife died 14 months ago. He is pervasively hopeless, unable to enjoy any activities, and has major depressive symptoms. Diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Distinguish MDD from PCBD by recognizing pervasive anhedonia and hopelessness extending beyond the deceased.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer: B) Major depressive disorder
body: Pervasive hopelessness and inability to enjoy anything point to MDD, rather than grief or PCBD where the mood focus remains mainly on the deceased.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: PCBD lacks pervasive global anhedonia into all aspects of life. Normal grief lasts <12 months and is hopeful. Adjustment disorder is not used for bereavement.

questions.retrievalTag: Hospitalize if BMI or E-lytes Extreme < 15
questions.reviewPearl: Anorexia nervosa severity is categorized by BMI, whereas Bulimia nervosa is categorized by the frequency of compensatory behaviors.
questions.stem: A 16-year-old girl with a BMI of 14 presents with amenorrhea, cold intolerance, and bradycardia. She firmly believes she is overweight. What is the first step in management?
questions.educationalObjective: Recognize the indications for acute medical hospitalization in extreme anorexia nervosa.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer: C) Hospitalization for refeeding
body: Anorexia nervosa patients with extreme BMI (<15) or severe signs like bradycardia and electrolyte shifts require immediate hospitalization for forced feeds.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: SSRIs and CBT are adjuncts used later. Bupropion is strictly contraindicated in eating disorders due to seizure risk.

questions.retrievalTag: BULIMIA NERVOSA Tx: SSRI (fluoxetine), CBT
questions.reviewPearl: Self-induced vomiting in bulimia frequently causes metabolic alkalosis, hypokalemia, dorsal hand scars, and dental erosion.
questions.stem: A 20-year-old female with a normal BMI reports binge eating followed by self-induced vomiting twice a week for 4 months. She has dorsal hand scars. Treatment?
questions.educationalObjective: Identify Bulimia Nervosa and select its primary outpatient treatment consisting of Fluoxetine and CBT.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer: A) Fluoxetine and CBT
body: Bulimia nervosa features binge/purge cycles with a normal or overweight BMI. It is treated with Cognitive Behavioral Therapy and SSRIs like fluoxetine.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Bupropion lowers the seizure threshold. Inpatient weight restoration is for anorexia with low BMI. Haloperidol is not indicated.

questions.retrievalTag: Bupropion causes seizures Bulimia NEVER
questions.reviewPearl: Never give bupropion to bulimics, as the electrolyte abnormalities from purging already predispose them to seizures.
questions.stem: A patient with bulimia nervosa is seeking medication for concurrent smoking cessation. Which medication is strictly contraindicated?
questions.educationalObjective: Identify bupropion as strictly contraindicated in bulimia nervosa.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer: B) Bupropion
body: Bupropion is an atypical antidepressant that lowers the seizure threshold and is strictly contraindicated in bulimia due to an unacceptably high risk of seizures.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Fluoxetine is the treatment of choice for bulimia. Varenicline and Sertraline do not carry this specific absolute contraindication in eating disorders.

questions.retrievalTag: Binge-Eating Disorder It's bulimia, except there's no purge
questions.reviewPearl: Binge-eating disorder patients are typically overweight or obese because they do not utilize purging or caloric restriction. eftab720 tightenfactor0 eftab720 tightenfactor0 Document 7: Antidepressants, Antianxiety, Mood Stabilizers
questions.stem: An overweight 35-year-old man eats unusually large amounts of food rapidly until uncomfortably full, feeling distressed afterward. He never uses laxatives or vomits. Diagnosis?
questions.educationalObjective: Distinguish binge-eating disorder from bulimia based on the absence of compensatory purging behaviors.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer: B) Binge-eating disorder
body: Binge-eating disorder involves recurrent binges causing distress, but crucially lacks the inappropriate compensatory behaviors (purging, fasting) seen in bulimia.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Bulimia nervosa requires compensatory behaviors. Anorexia features severe underweight. Prader-Willi is a genetic childhood syndrome causing hyperphagia.

questions.retrievalTag: Bupropion Smoking cessation No weight gain
questions.reviewPearl: Mirtazapine is an excellent choice for depressed patients who are malnourished because it acts as an appetite stimulant.
questions.stem: A patient with major depression wants a medication that will also help him quit smoking without causing weight gain. Which is the best choice?
questions.educationalObjective: Select bupropion for a depressed patient desiring concurrent smoking cessation and avoidance of weight gain.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer: C) Bupropion
body: Bupropion is an atypical antidepressant that aids in smoking cessation, treats depression effectively, and importantly prevents weight gain.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Mirtazapine stimulates appetite and causes weight gain. Paroxetine is an SSRI with no smoking benefit. Trazodone is primarily a sleep aid.

questions.retrievalTag: Lithium Causes nephro DI
questions.reviewPearl: Lithium has a narrow therapeutic index and requires regular monitoring to prevent nephrotoxicity, ataxia, and encephalopathy.
questions.stem: A bipolar patient stabilized on long-term medication develops severe polyuria and hypernatremia. Which medication is most likely responsible?
questions.educationalObjective: Recognize nephrogenic diabetes insipidus as a major side effect of Lithium therapy.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer: B) Lithium
body: Lithium toxicity or chronic use causes nephrotoxicity leading to nephrogenic diabetes insipidus, which presents with polyuria and hypernatremia.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Valproic acid causes thrombocytopenia and pancreatitis. Quetiapine causes weight gain and QTc prolongation. Lamotrigine causes rash/SJS.

questions.retrievalTag: giving a drug at least 6 weeks... before switching
questions.reviewPearl: Remember the "666" rule: 6 weeks minimum trial, 6 months maintenance therapy, and a 6-week washout if switching to an MAOI.
questions.stem: A patient started an SSRI 2 weeks ago for MDD but reports no improvement in mood. What is the most appropriate next step?

answerChoices.label: C
answerChoices.text: Increase the dose immediately to maximum

answerChoices.label: D
answerChoices.text: Continue the medication and follow up in 4 weeks

questions.educationalObjective: Manage SSRI trials by recognizing the 6-week latency period required for maximal therapeutic efficacy.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer: D) Continue the medication and follow up in 4 weeks
body: Antidepressants require an adequate trial of at least 4 to 6 weeks at a therapeutic dose to allow neuronal plasticity before efficacy can be judged.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Switching, augmenting, or aggressively maxing the dose at 2 weeks are premature interventions that do not allow the drug sufficient time to work.

questions.retrievalTag: Valproate Teratogen (spina bifida)
questions.reviewPearl: Both lithium and valproate are potent teratogens, severely limiting their utility in women with bipolar disorder who desire pregnancy or refuse contraception. eftab720 tightenfactor0 eftab720 tightenfactor0 Document 8: Anxiety Disorders
questions.stem: A female bipolar patient refuses Lithium. She is prescribed valproate. Which congenital defect must she be warned about if she becomes pregnant?
questions.educationalObjective: Identify spina bifida as the major teratogenic risk associated with valproate therapy.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer: B) Spina bifida
body: Valproate is a known teratogen that causes neural tube defects, specifically spina bifida, limiting its utility in pregnant women.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Ebstein anomaly is caused by Lithium. Cleft palate is associated with carbamazepine. Renal agenesis can be caused by ACE inhibitors.

questions.retrievalTag: GAD Psychotherapy > SSRI
questions.reviewPearl: Benzodiazepines should be used to treat acute panic attacks only; their use for chronic anxiety like GAD risks severe dependence and withdrawal.
questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman reports 8 months of constant, daily worry about her finances, health, and relationships, accompanied by muscle tension. Best long-term management?
questions.educationalObjective: Diagnose Generalized Anxiety Disorder and select Psychotherapy plus an SSRI as the definitive long-term treatment.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer: B) Psychotherapy and SSRI
body: Generalized Anxiety Disorder (GAD) involves >6 months of constant worry with somatic symptoms; it is treated long-term with psychotherapy and SSRIs.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Benzodiazepines are explicitly the wrong answer for chronic GAD anxiety. Haloperidol is an antipsychotic. Reassurance ignores a valid pathology.

questions.retrievalTag: Panic Attacks BZD
questions.reviewPearl: Long-term management of panic disorder relies on SSRIs and CBT to prevent attacks and avoid benzodiazepine dependence.
questions.stem: A healthy 24-year-old woman presents to the ED with sudden palpitations, chest pain, and fear of dying lasting 20 minutes. Troponins and ECG are normal. Immediate treatment?
questions.educationalObjective: Use benzodiazepines to acutely abort a panic attack once medical emergencies are fully ruled out.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer: B) Benzodiazepine
body: Acute panic attacks are sudden and severe. After ruling out medical emergencies, abortive therapy with a short-acting benzodiazepine is indicated in the acute setting.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: SSRIs and CBT are for long-term prevention, not acute chemical abortion. Beta-blockers are primarily used for performance anxiety.

questions.retrievalTag: -Blockers Performance anxiety
questions.reviewPearl: Beta-blockers lower the heart rate and control physical symptoms, making them ideal PRN medications for specific social phobias like public speaking.
questions.stem: A law student develops intense tachycardia, sweating, and trembling only when standing in front of the class to speak. He avoids public speaking entirely. Best PRN treatment?
questions.educationalObjective: Prescribe beta-blockers as needed for performance anxiety/stage fright.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer: C) Propranolol
body: Performance anxiety (a social phobia variant) is effectively treated with as-needed beta-blockers like propranolol to control autonomic symptoms before an event.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Benzodiazepines cause cognitive sedation. SSRIs are for chronic daily anxiety. Valproate is a mood stabilizer for bipolar disorder.

questions.retrievalTag: Systemic desensitization (slower, more effective over time
questions.reviewPearl: Flooding is used when time is restricted, burning out the anxiety response quickly but with less long-term durability than desensitization.
questions.stem: A patient has an intense, irrational fear of spiders causing significant impairment. She wishes to overcome this long-term. Best highly effective psychological therapy?
questions.educationalObjective: Select systemic desensitization as the most effective long-term behavioral treatment for specific phobias.

explanationSections.heading: Correct Answer: C) Systemic desensitization
body: Systemic desensitization involving gradual exposure while controlling anxiety is the best, most durable long-term treatment for specific phobias.

explanationSections.heading: Incorrect Answer Explanation
body: Flooding is faster but less effective over time. Psychoanalysis and hypnosis are not first-line behavioral techniques for specific phobias.
